label: Pesher Habakkuk (Commentary on Habakkuk) — Complete
summary_plain: Pesher Habakkuk is a continuous commentary on the prophetic book of Habakkuk, interpreting its words as prophecy about the community’s own time. The Kittim are the instrument of YHWH’s judgment — they come from afar, swift and violent, conquering all lands. The Teacher of Righteousness received from YHWH’s mouth the interpretation of the prophets’ words. The Wicked Priest pursued the Teacher of Righteousness to swallow him up on the Day of Atonement. The righteous shall live by his faithfulness — this applies to those who observe the Torah in the house of Yehudah, whom YHWH will deliver because of their toil and their faithfulness to the Teacher of Righteousness.
summary_scholarly: 1QpHab (Pesher Habakkuk) was among the first seven scrolls discovered in Cave 1 in 1947. It is the best-preserved pesher and the most important text for understanding the Qumran hermeneutical method. The commentary covers Habakkuk 1-2 verse by verse. The Kittim are identified as Rome — they sacrifice to their standards, they come from the coastlands, they conquer by systematic military force. The Teacher of Righteousness is YHWH’s chosen interpreter of the prophets. The Wicked Priest is a Jerusalem priest who initially walked in truth but was corrupted by wealth and power. Habakkuk 2:4 (‘the righteous shall live by his faithfulness’) is interpreted as applying specifically to Torah-observers faithful to the Teacher. The pesher method assumes that the prophets wrote about the future — the community’s own time — and that YHWH revealed the meaning to the Teacher of Righteousness.

key_terms.definition: In the Dead Sea Scrolls, the Kittim are Rome. They come from the coastlands of the sea, conquer systematically, and sacrifice to their military standards. In Pesher Habakkuk, they are YHWH’s instrument of judgment against the corrupt Jerusalem establishment.

The ‘Wicked Priest’ — a Jerusalem priest who began in truth but was corrupted by wealth and power. He pursued the Teacher of Righteousness and tried to destroy him on the Day of Atonement. His identity is debated but likely refers to a Hasmonean high priest.

fill_blank.prompt: YHWH told Habakkuk to write down the things that would come upon the last generation, but the completion of the end he did not make known to him. Its pesher concerns the Teacher of Righteousness to whom YHWH made known all the mysteries of the words of his servants the ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: Its pesher concerns the Teacher of Righteousness to whom YHWH made known all the mysteries of the words of his servants the prophets.

fill_blank.prompt: The righteous shall live by his ______. Its pesher concerns all observers of the Torah in the house of Yehudah.
fill_blank.source_quote: The righteous shall live by his faithfulness. Its pesher concerns all observers of the Torah in the house of Yehudah, whom YHWH will deliver because of their toil and their faithfulness to the Teacher of Righteousness.

multiple_choice.question: What did the Wicked Priest do to the Teacher of Righteousness?
options: He appointed him as high priest
He pursued him to swallow him up on the Day of Atonement
He sent him into exile in Babylon
He invited him to a feast in the Temple
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Wicked Priest pursued the Teacher of Righteousness to swallow him up on his day of fasting, on the Day of Atonement.

multiple_choice.question: Who are the Kittim in Pesher Habakkuk?
options: The Romans, who come from the coastlands and sacrifice to their standards
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Kittim come from the coastlands of the sea, swift and terrible, conquering all peoples. They sacrifice to their standards and worship their weapons of war.

faq.question: What does Pesher Habakkuk reveal about the Kittim as Rome?
faq.answer: Pesher Habakkuk identifies the Kittim with specific detail: they come from the coastlands of the sea, they conquer systematically, they sacrifice to their military standards, they worship their weapons of war, they impose tribute on the nations annually. Every detail matches Rome. The identification of the Kittim as Rome is critical for the Great Eraser framework because it demonstrates that the Qumran community recognized Rome as the current instrument of oppression — the same Rome that would destroy the Temple in 70 CE, scatter the covenant people, and establish the institutional infrastructure (roads, administration, state religion) upon which later erasure mechanisms would be built. The pesher’s identification of Rome is not abstract — it is a covenant community’s documented assessment of the empire that would, within decades, destroy them.

faq.question: Why is Habakkuk 2:4 — ‘the righteous shall live by his faithfulness’ — important in the Dead Sea Scrolls?
faq.answer: Pesher Habakkuk interprets ‘the righteous shall live by his faithfulness’ as applying specifically to those who observe the Torah and remain faithful to the Teacher of Righteousness. This interpretation is pre-Christian and pre-rabbinic. It roots ‘faithfulness’ (emunah) in Torah observance, not in abstract belief. The Great Eraser has appropriated this verse into frameworks that disconnect it from its Torah-observance context. The Qumran pesher preserves the original interpretive tradition: faithfulness means doing the works of the Torah under persecution, trusting YHWH when the Wicked Priest attacks, maintaining covenant practice when the institution fails. The verse is not about theology in the abstract. It is about covenant people keeping the Torah when keeping the Torah could get them killed.
